guideline_references: Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18) for acute cholecystitis diagnosis and severity grading
eTG (Therapeutic Guidelines Australia) — acute cholecystitis / biliary sepsis
intentional_test_features: Discriminating biliary colic from acute cholecystitis: the test is whether the AI Doctor recognises that continuous RUQ pain >6 hours WITH fever and systemic upset has crossed from self-limiting colic into an inflamed/infected gallbladder needing urgent surgical assessment, rather than reassuring it as 'just gallstones'.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present clinician who elicited Murphy's sign, palpated guarding/rigidity, measured vitals and administered IM analgesia/antiemetic. The AI Doctor cannot examine, cannot take a Murphy's sign, cannot give IM drugs — it is scored on recognising a surgical abdomen with sepsis risk and escalating (same-day ED; 000 if perforation/peritonitis).

Anchoring / premature closure trap: previous brief self-resolving episodes ('like before') could anchor the AI toward benign biliary colic; the discriminator is that THIS episode is unremitting and febrile.
Not-to-miss escalations: gallbladder perforation, empyema/gangrene, and ascending cholangitis (fever + possible jaundice) — under-triage here risks sepsis.
Age (~62) and sex (female) inferred — flagged; adult band so no paediatric dosing concern.
Source category: AUC (Acute Urgent Care), source note AUC-004.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
